text: This is a small video presentation of a lab mucosal appendectomy for a fifty-four year old Rayleigh with mild pain in the RAF with a raised CEA with colonoscopy normal and shared mild tenderness in the right iliac fossa and this is an MRI of the abdomen showing a large cystic lesion present in the right iliac fossa adjacent to the cecum in a retrocecal position. This is a well-defined oblong cystic lesion of a maximum dimension of six centimeters, features suggestive of a mucosal of appendix. There's no soft tissue components, no hemorrhage, and no evidence of neural thickening, no features of a malignancy within this. We did a plain CT on top of this MRI and showed pretty much the same findings with a distance from the sacral wall to the lesion of only six millimeters.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel to remove peri-appendiceal adhesions, revealing a large cystic lesion in the right iliac fossa during the laparoscopic exploration.

The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix for later attention. The meso-appendix is completely divided, and the appendix is skeletonized. A linear stapler is used to divide a portion of the cecum, ensuring the division line is at least one centimeter away from the tumor. The surgical procedure is completed following this step.

The surgeon carefully bags and retrieves the specimen, ensuring it does not rupture. A small incision is made at the left iliac region to facilitate the removal of the specimen. The specimen is identified as a low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm.

Margins were uninvolved and the patient's repeat CEA was normal. On further checking of literature, we found there have been certain cases like this, where a mucinous cystadenoma is present in the elevated CEA, although the colonoscopy is quite novel.
